sourceRefs.fallbackText: Nigel Tao 29 September 2011
sourceRefs.textQuote: Nigel Tao 29 September 2011

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Package image/draw defines only one operation: drawing a source image onto a destination image, through an optional mask image. This one operation is surprisingly versatile and can perform a number of common image manipulation tasks elegantly and efficiently.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Package image/draw defines only one operation: drawing a source image onto a destination image, through an optional mask image. This one operation is surprisingly versatile and can perform a number of common image manipu

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Composition is performed pixel by pixel in the style of the Plan 9 graphics library and the X Render extension. The model is based on the classic “Compositing Digital Images” paper by Porter and Duff, with an additional mask parameter: dst = (src IN mask) OP dst . For a fully opaque mask, this reduces to the original Porter-Duff formula: dst = src OP dst . I
sourceRefs.textQuote: Composition is performed pixel by pixel in the style of the Plan 9 graphics library and the X Render extension. The model is based on the classic “Compositing Digital Images” paper by Porter and Duff, with an additional

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The Porter-Duff paper presented 12 different composition operators , but with an explicit mask, only 2 of these are needed in practice: source-over-destination and source. In Go, these operators are represented by the Over and Src constants. The Over operator performs the natural layering of a source image over a destination image: the change to the destinat
sourceRefs.textQuote: The Porter-Duff paper presented 12 different composition operators , but with an explicit mask, only 2 of these are needed in practice: source-over-destination and source. In Go, these operators are represented by the Ov

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Composition requires associating destination pixels with source and mask pixels. Obviously, this requires destination, source and mask images, and a composition operator, but it also requires specifying what rectangle of each image to use. Not every drawing should write to the entire destination: when updating an animating image, it is more efficient to only
sourceRefs.textQuote: Composition requires associating destination pixels with source and mask pixels. Obviously, this requires destination, source and mask images, and a composition operator, but it also requires specifying what rectangle of

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The DrawMask function takes seven arguments, but an explicit mask and mask-point are usually unnecessary, so the Draw function takes five:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The DrawMask function takes seven arguments, but an explicit mask and mask-point are usually unnecessary, so the Draw function takes five:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: // Draw calls DrawMask with a nil mask. func Draw(dst Image, r image.Rectangle, src image.Image, sp image.Point, op Op) func DrawMask(dst Image, r image.Rectangle, src image.Image, sp image.Point, mask image.Image, mp image.Point, op Op)
sourceRefs.textQuote: // Draw calls DrawMask with a nil mask. func Draw(dst Image, r image.Rectangle, src image.Image, sp image.Point, op Op) func DrawMask(dst Image, r image.Rectangle, src image.Image, sp image.Point, mask image.Image, mp im

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The destination image must be mutable, so the image/draw package defines a draw.Image interface which has a Set method.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The destination image must be mutable, so the image/draw package defines a draw.Image interface which has a Set method.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: type Image interface { image.Image Set(x, y int, c color.Color) }
sourceRefs.textQuote: type Image interface { image.Image Set(x, y int, c color.Color) }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To fill a rectangle with a solid color, use an image.Uniform source. The ColorImage type re-interprets a Color as a practically infinite-sized Image of that color. For those familiar with the design of Plan 9’s draw library, there is no need for an explicit “repeat bit” in Go’s slice-based image types; the concept is subsumed by Uniform .
sourceRefs.textQuote: To fill a rectangle with a solid color, use an image.Uniform source. The ColorImage type re-interprets a Color as a practically infinite-sized Image of that color. For those familiar with the design of Plan 9’s draw libr

sourceRefs.fallbackText: // image.ZP is the zero point -- the origin. draw.Draw(dst, r, &image.Uniform{c}, image.ZP, draw.Src)
sourceRefs.textQuote: // image.ZP is the zero point -- the origin. draw.Draw(dst, r, &image.Uniform{c}, image.ZP, draw.Src)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To initialize a new image to all-blue:
sourceRefs.textQuote: To initialize a new image to all-blue:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: m := image.NewRGBA(image.Rect(0, 0, 640, 480)) blue := color.RGBA{0, 0, 255, 255} draw.Draw(m, m.Bounds(), &image.Uniform{blue}, image.ZP, draw.Src)
sourceRefs.textQuote: m := image.NewRGBA(image.Rect(0, 0, 640, 480)) blue := color.RGBA{0, 0, 255, 255} draw.Draw(m, m.Bounds(), &image.Uniform{blue}, image.ZP, draw.Src)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To reset an image to transparent (or black, if the destination image’s color model cannot represent transparency), use image.Transparent , which is an image.Uniform :
sourceRefs.textQuote: To reset an image to transparent (or black, if the destination image’s color model cannot represent transparency), use image.Transparent , which is an image.Uniform :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: draw.Draw(m, m.Bounds(), image.Transparent, image.ZP, draw.Src)
sourceRefs.textQuote: draw.Draw(m, m.Bounds(), image.Transparent, image.ZP, draw.Src)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To copy from a rectangle sr in the source image to a rectangle starting at a point dp in the destination, convert the source rectangle into the destination image’s co-ordinate space:
sourceRefs.textQuote: To copy from a rectangle sr in the source image to a rectangle starting at a point dp in the destination, convert the source rectangle into the destination image’s co-ordinate space:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: r := image.Rectangle{dp, dp.Add(sr.Size())} draw.Draw(dst, r, src, sr.Min, draw.Src)
sourceRefs.textQuote: r := image.Rectangle{dp, dp.Add(sr.Size())} draw.Draw(dst, r, src, sr.Min, draw.Src)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: r := sr.Sub(sr.Min).Add(dp) draw.Draw(dst, r, src, sr.Min, draw.Src)
sourceRefs.textQuote: r := sr.Sub(sr.Min).Add(dp) draw.Draw(dst, r, src, sr.Min, draw.Src)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To copy the entire source image, use sr = src.Bounds() .
sourceRefs.textQuote: To copy the entire source image, use sr = src.Bounds() .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Scrolling an image is just copying an image to itself, with different destination and source rectangles. Overlapping destination and source images are perfectly valid, just as Go’s built-in copy function can handle overlapping destination and source slices. To scroll an image m by 20 pixels:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Scrolling an image is just copying an image to itself, with different destination and source rectangles. Overlapping destination and source images are perfectly valid, just as Go’s built-in copy function can handle overl

sourceRefs.fallbackText: b := m.Bounds() p := image.Pt(0, 20) // Note that even though the second argument is b, // the effective rectangle is smaller due to clipping. draw.Draw(m, b, m, b.Min.Add(p), draw.Src) dirtyRect := b.Intersect(image.Rect(b.Min.X, b.Max.Y-20, b.Max.X, b.Max.Y))
sourceRefs.textQuote: b := m.Bounds() p := image.Pt(0, 20) // Note that even though the second argument is b, // the effective rectangle is smaller due to clipping. draw.Draw(m, b, m, b.Min.Add(p), draw.Src) dirtyRect := b.Intersect(image.Rec

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The result of decoding an image format might not be an image.RGBA : decoding a GIF results in an image.Paletted , decoding a JPEG results in a ycbcr.YCbCr , and the result of decoding a PNG depends on the image data. To convert any image to an image.RGBA :
sourceRefs.textQuote: The result of decoding an image format might not be an image.RGBA : decoding a GIF results in an image.Paletted , decoding a JPEG results in a ycbcr.YCbCr , and the result of decoding a PNG depends on the image data. To

sourceRefs.fallbackText: b := src.Bounds() m := image.NewRGBA(image.Rect(0, 0, b.Dx(), b.Dy())) draw.Draw(m, m.Bounds(), src, b.Min, draw.Src)
sourceRefs.textQuote: b := src.Bounds() m := image.NewRGBA(image.Rect(0, 0, b.Dx(), b.Dy())) draw.Draw(m, m.Bounds(), src, b.Min, draw.Src)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To draw an image through a circular mask with center p and radius r :
sourceRefs.textQuote: To draw an image through a circular mask with center p and radius r :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: type circle struct { p image.Point r int } func (c *circle) ColorModel() color.Model { return color.AlphaModel } func (c *circle) Bounds() image.Rectangle { return image.Rect(c.p.X-c.r, c.p.Y-c.r, c.p.X+c.r, c.p.Y+c.r) } func (c *circle) At(x, y int) color.Color { xx, yy, rr := float64(x-c.p.X)+0.5, float64(y-c.p.Y)+0.5, float64(c.r) if xx*xx+yy*yy < rr*rr {
sourceRefs.textQuote: type circle struct { p image.Point r int } func (c *circle) ColorModel() color.Model { return color.AlphaModel } func (c *circle) Bounds() image.Rectangle { return image.Rect(c.p.X-c.r, c.p.Y-c.r, c.p.X+c.r, c.p.Y+c.r) }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To draw a font glyph in blue starting from a point p , draw with an image.ColorImage source and an image.Alpha mask . For simplicity, we aren’t performing any sub-pixel positioning or rendering, or correcting for a font’s height above a baseline.
sourceRefs.textQuote: To draw a font glyph in blue starting from a point p , draw with an image.ColorImage source and an image.Alpha mask . For simplicity, we aren’t performing any sub-pixel positioning or rendering, or correcting for a font’

sourceRefs.fallbackText: src := &image.Uniform{color.RGBA{0, 0, 255, 255}} mask := theGlyphImageForAFont() mr := theBoundsFor(glyphIndex) draw.DrawMask(dst, mr.Sub(mr.Min).Add(p), src, image.ZP, mask, mr.Min, draw.Over)
sourceRefs.textQuote: src := &image.Uniform{color.RGBA{0, 0, 255, 255}} mask := theGlyphImageForAFont() mr := theBoundsFor(glyphIndex) draw.DrawMask(dst, mr.Sub(mr.Min).Add(p), src, image.ZP, mask, mr.Min, draw.Over)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The image/draw package implementation demonstrates how to provide an image manipulation function that is both general purpose, yet efficient for common cases.
